what_it_does: Executes every *_acceptance suite as a standalone subprocess via Suite.suite_health(), classifies each result as green, needs-live-dep, or red, and fails loudly if any suite is genuinely red. It excludes itself to avoid recursion.
observations: The file is a top-level gate script rather than a traditional unit test.
It imports project-specific runtime classes: NodeRegistry, FsStore, and Suite.
It discovers nodes from company/nodes and creates a temporary FsStore.
It calls suite.suite_health() and prints a count of green / needs-live-dep / red suites.
It ends with an assert report['all_green'] that aborts with a detailed message on red suites.
The docstring documents a 2026-06-08 incident where three red/false-green suites slipped per-build gates.
It treats 'needs-live-dep' as a documented skip, not a code failure.
It is explicitly described as slow and intended for pre-merge / pre-deploy / periodic use.
standouts: The core idea is catching 'silent-red' suites that per-build affected-suite logic never runs.
It requires every acceptance suite to be runnable standalone, exposing invocation-dependent false greens.
It includes a recursion guard so the gate does not run itself.
The assert message explains the failure class and remediation path.
conventions: Heavy docstring with historical rationale and threat model.
Top-level procedural script with imports, setup, execution, and assertion.
Human-readable console output before the final assertion.
Uses project abstractions (NodeRegistry, FsStore, Suite) for discovery and execution.
concerns: The script runs side effects and asserts at import time with no __main__ guard, so importing the module executes the gate.
It mutates sys.path to include the project root.
It relies entirely on the unshown behavior of Suite.suite_health() for discovery, subprocess execution, and skip classification.
Running every acceptance suite as a subprocess at import time could be slow and surprising in test discovery.
notes: A standing all-green gate script that runs every acceptance suite standalone to catch silent reds before merge or deploy.
questions: How does Suite.suite_health() discover suites and decide the 'needs-live-dep' classification?
What naming convention or metadata marks a file as an *_acceptance suite?
How and where is this gate invoked in CI or release workflow?
Why is there no if __name__ == '__main__' guard to protect import-time execution?
What exactly constitutes a documented live-dependency skip versus a red failure?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose matches the actual behavior: it discovers acceptance suites, runs them standalone, and fails loudly on real reds while allowing documented live-dependency skips.
apparent_themes: Standing gate versus per-build affected-suite gate.
Silent failure detection and regression prevention.
Documented skips for external live dependencies.
intention_signals: The file is trying to prevent acceptance suites from rotting undetected by running each one in isolation and surfacing real failures that per-build selection logic misses.
novelty: A meta-suite gate whose purpose is to verify that every other acceptance suite is independently runnable and green, with explicit classification of live-dependency skips.
contribution: Provides a periodic, fail-loud health check that every acceptance suite passes standalone, helping ensure per-build gaps do not hide broken or invocation-dependent suites.
summary_for_embedding: A pre-merge standing gate script that discovers and runs every *_acceptance suite standalone, classifies results as green/needs-live-dep/red, and fails loudly on real reds.
intention_for_embedding: To catch acceptance suites that are silently red or falsely green due to invocation dependencies before merging or deploying.
